sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Julia Jones, is a 25-year-old female presenting for evaluation of breast reduction due to symptomatic macromastia.
She has attempted nonsurgical treatments with minimal relief and is an ideal candidate for bilateral reduction mammoplasty.
She reports experiencing back pain, neck pain, and bra strap indentations in her shoulders.
She has been considering a breast reduction for approximately 6-7 years.
She is planning to switch to her husband's insurance plan, which may be Blue Cross, but is unsure.
She has a history of family breast cancer, with her mother being diagnosed at 40 years old, and has been advised to start mammograms at 30 years old.
She is currently taking propranolol for migraines and has a history of occasional wine consumption.
She is planning to pursue a degree in psychology.
She has been going to the chiropractor for back pain and has lost 10 pounds in the past few months, with a goal of losing 20 more pounds.
Past medical history: The patient has a family history of breast cancer, with her mother being diagnosed at 40 years old.
She has been advised to start mammograms at 30 years old.
Review of systems: The patient reports experiencing back pain, neck pain, and numbness or tingling in the hands, as well as nipple pain.
She denies experiencing rashes or breast tenderness.
Current medications: Propranolol 1 tablet daily for migraines.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's breasts are asymmetrical with severe ptosis bilaterally.
There is no nipple retraction or discharge bilaterally, and no breast tenderness, masses, or axillary lymphadenopathy is palpable.
Test Results: Not mentioned.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is a 25-year-old female with symptomatic macromastia, ideal candidate for bilateral reduction mammoplasty.
Problem list: Macromastia; active.
Plan:
I plan to submit the patient's case to the insurance company for approval after she has switched to her husband's plan in July.
I will take photos of the patient's breasts and document her symptoms to send to the insurance company.
Once approved, we will schedule the surgery and meet with the patient again to answer any last-minute questions and review instructions in more detail.
Follow-up: The patient will notify the office when she has switched to her new insurance plan, and we will proceed with submitting the case for approval.
Once approved, we will schedule the surgery and meet with the patient again before the procedure.
